title: "Conformal Prediction"
bibliography: references\conformalprediction.bib
format: 
  docx:
    reference-doc: templates\packt-sample-chapter.docx
    toc: true  
    toc-depth: 4 
    number-section: true 
    toc-title: Contents
---


# 7

# Conformal prediction

```{python}
#| label: Chapter 7 libraries
#| message: false
#| echo: false
#| eval: true

import os
import sys
from pathlib import Path
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
from matplotlib.patches import FancyArrowPatch
from matplotlib.pyplot import figure

import matplotlib.colors as mcolors
from matplotlib.patches import Patch
plt.style.use('seaborn-v0_8')

import seaborn as sns
from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression
from sklearn.preprocessing import PolynomialFeatures


# Get working directory
cwd = os.getcwd()

# Get parent directory
parent_dir = os.path.dirname(cwd)

# Add parent directory to system path
sys.path.insert(0, parent_dir)


# Define palette
custom_palette = ["#000000", "#0072B2", "#D55E00","#009E73","#CC79A7", "#56B4E9","#E69F00"]
line_styles = ['-', '--', '-.', ':']

plt.rcParams['figure.figsize'] = (6, 12)
plt.rcParams['font.family'] = 'sans-serif'
plt.rcParams['font.sans-serif'] = ['Source Sans Pro', 'Arial']
plt.rcParams['font.size'] = 14
plt.rcParams['axes.labelsize'] = 14
plt.rcParams['lines.linewidth'] = 2
plt.rcParams['axes.titlesize'] = 18

# general settings
class CFG:
    data_folder = Path.cwd().parent / "data"
    img_dim1 = 12
    img_dim2 = 6
    fontsize = 18
    
    
# adjust the parameters for displayed figures    
plt.rcParams.update({'figure.figsize': (CFG.img_dim1,CFG.img_dim2)}) 
```

“Uncertainty is an uncomfortable position. But certainty is an absurd one.” ― Voltaire

All our predictions miss the mark to some degree, and that is fine - the old adage about all models being wrong is a cliché, right alongside the correlation and causation one. But it points to an important question, which is 'how wrong are we'? Translating to slightly more formal language (this is a book dealing with machine learning, after all), it is useful to think about forecasting in terms of point and interval forecasting: the former predicts a specific value, while the latter quantifies the error / uncertainty around it.

Under classical time series modelling the interval aspect is fairly straightforward to handle:
- ARIMA class of models rely on parametric assumptions, so deriving a confidence interval around point forecasts amounts to manipulating conditional distributions
  - with more general types of classic time series (e.g. non-Gaussian state space models) there are no closed formulas, but with Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampling distributions are possible to obtain
- Bayesian methods capture both parametric and sampling approaches as special cases (after a fashion)
- If these approaches fail, we could always bootstrap data - adjusting for to preserve temporal 
  dependence, such has in block bootstrapping.


While each of those approaches achieves some degree of success in different applications, there is always some fragmentation to the overall setup. This problem was exacerbated with the advent of machine learning methods, and deep learning afterwards: quantification of uncertainty in regression problems (of which, time series is a special form) became something of an afterthought in most applications - but the practical issues that motivated the study of forecast uncertainty did not go away. And as you will find in practice, uncertainty is an important factor in decision-making for many business problems.  

As in every good story, cometh the hour, cometh the man: the year was 1998, and the men were Alex Gammerman, Vladimir Vovk and Vladimir Vapnik. By modifying the original Support Vector Machine (SVM) algorithm, they demonstrated a method of assigning confidence scores to predictions of a classifier. Their paper "Learning by transduction" [@gammermanLearningTransduction2013] laid the foundation on which conformal prediction was built.

Conformal prediction (CP) is a method for constructing prediction intervals in machine learning, moving from point to interval forecasts. Under mild assumptions, conformal intervals are guaranteed to contain the true value of the next observation within a certain probability: this feature makes CP particularly useful in situations where reliable predictions with uncertainty bounds are required. In this chapter, we introduce CP as a general approach to prediction uncertainty quantification for time series, which will be useful throughout the remainder of the book.

In this chapter, we will:

* Build an intuitive understanding of conformal prediction through worked regression 
  examples
* Learn how non-conformity scores, conformity ladders, and quantiles create prediction 
  intervals
* Understand the split conformal method and why it solves the computational problems of 
  full conformal prediction
* Explore different splitting strategies: bootstrap, cross-conformal, jackknife, and CQR
* Apply EnbPI conformal intervals to a PyTorch forecasting model using MAPIE

## Uncertainty quantification

Inherent to any forecast predictions is an element of uncertainty as a consequence of factors that are unknown to us, along with difficulties in measuring and modelling our data. In general, we can say that any model we build is done so with incomplete data to create approximations of reality, which will produce predictions that are influenced by many sources of uncertainty.

When you create a supervised regression model, you will separate data into exogenous variables ($X$) and dependent variables ($Y$), so you can regress exogenous variables, such as price, on dependent values, like sales. You are attempting to capture ‘patterns’ in training data, when passing this data through ‘models’, that may be used to predict $Y$ (sales in this case) for new points $X$. If you have $X$ price of £0.6, your model should predict a value of sales ($\hat{Y}$), based on the learnt model parameters; this is a point prediction. While useful, point predictions do not provide us with any understanding of potential distances between themselves and actual values ($Y$). This is a fundamental problem from a business perspective, as modelled predictions can be terrible, which can undermine confidence in modelling systems. This is especially true where modelled predictions vary from run to run, as most GBDTs and neural networks do when we do not control the seeding of random processes. 

```{python}
#| label: fig-point-prediction-error
#| fig-cap: "Point prediction relative to actual value. The vertical arrow shows the distance (error) between the model's prediction and the true observed value."
#| fig-alt: "Scatter plot showing a linear regression line with a predicted point and true value, and an arrow indicating the absolute error between them"
#| message: false
#| echo: false
#| eval: true

# Create linear data
x = np.linspace(0.1, 0.9, 100)
y = 2 * x + 1  

# Predicted point
x_pred = 0.6
y_pred = 2 * x_pred + 1

# True value for demonstration
y_true = 2.5

# Create plot
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 6))
ax.plot(x, y, label='Linear prediction (Model)', color=custom_palette[0], linestyle='--')  # Model line
ax.scatter([x_pred], [y_pred], color=custom_palette[1], zorder=5)  # Predicted point
ax.scatter([x_pred], [y_true + 0.12], color=custom_palette[2], zorder=5)  # True value

# Annotating true and predicted value
ax.annotate('Predicted Value', (x_pred, y_pred), textcoords="offset points", xytext=(-57,-3), ha='center', color=custom_palette[1])
ax.annotate('True Value', (x_pred, y_true), textcoords="offset points", xytext=(-42,15), ha='center', color=custom_palette[2])

# Draw error arrow
ax.arrow(x_pred, y_pred, 0, y_true - y_pred, head_width=0.02, head_length=0.1, fc=custom_palette[1], ec=custom_palette[2])

# Labeling axes and title
ax.set_xlabel('X')
ax.set_ylabel('Y')
ax.set_title('Point Prediction relative to actual value')
ax.legend()

plt.grid(True)
plt.show()
```

Quantifying the likelihood of predictions being correct, or not, guides our judgements about the degree a model and its predictions may be trusted. Of course, this is relative to our tolerance of risk, and what to do if a model does not meet expectations. 

Uncertainty quantification (UQ) aims to help us estimate the probability of predictions actually being observed. We can broadly categorise uncertainty as either aleatoric or epistemic.
1.	Aleatoric uncertainty – caused by intrinsic randomness and variability within data, as a feature of the systems we investigate i.e. stock market prices. 
2.	Epistemic uncertainty – comes from incomplete knowledge/understanding. 

These concepts are demonstrated in the following diagram [inspired by @manokhinPracticalGuideApplied2023]:

* The left cluster exhibits high aleatoric uncertainty due to pronounced randomness from a linear model, while the right has low aleatoric uncertainty due to the regularities in data. 
* Additionally, we see voids with no data, signifying high epistemic uncertainty: where we lack observations, our knowledge of the underlying function is incomplete.


```{python}
#| label: fig-uncertainty-types
#| fig-cap: "Aleatoric and epistemic uncertainty. The left cluster shows high aleatoric uncertainty from noise around a linear model. The right cluster shows low aleatoric uncertainty. Voids with no data represent high epistemic uncertainty."
#| fig-alt: "Scatter plot showing two data clusters with differing noise levels and annotated regions of high epistemic uncertainty"
#| message: false
#| echo: false
#| eval: true

# Synthetic data
np.random.seed(0)
x_high_aleatoric = np.linspace(-4, -2, 50)
y_high_aleatoric = 0.3 * x_high_aleatoric + np.random.normal(loc=0, scale=1.5, size=50)

x_low_aleatoric = np.linspace(2, 4, 50)
y_low_aleatoric = 0.5 * x_low_aleatoric + np.random.normal(loc=0, scale=0.3, size=50)

x_line = np.linspace(-10, 10, 200)
y_line = 0.5 * x_line  # Real function

plt.style.use('seaborn-v0_8-ticks')
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(CFG.img_dim1, CFG.img_dim2))
ax.scatter(x_high_aleatoric, y_high_aleatoric, color=custom_palette[5], label='High Aleatoric \n Uncertainty')
ax.scatter(x_low_aleatoric, y_low_aleatoric, color=custom_palette[6], alpha=0.6, label='Aleatoric \n Uncertainty')

# Plotting real function
ax.plot(x_line, y_line, 'k--', label='Real Function')

# Annotating areas of uncertainty
ax.annotate('High Aleatoric \n Uncertainty', xy=(-3.0, 1.5), xytext=(-7.5, 4.5),
            arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle='->', lw=1.5, color='black'), fontsize=14)
ax.annotate('Low Aleatoric \n Uncertainty', xy=(2.5, 1.5), xytext=(2.5, 4.5),
            arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle='->', lw=1.5, color='black'), fontsize=14)
ax.annotate('High Epistemic \n Uncertainty', xy=(-7.5, -4), xytext=(-5, -5),
            arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle='->', lw=1.5, color='black'), fontsize=14)
ax.annotate('High Epistemic \n Uncertainty', xy=(6.5, 3), xytext=(5, -5),
            arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle='->', lw=1.5, color='black'), fontsize=14)

# Labeling and aesthetics
ax.set_xlabel('X')
ax.set_ylabel('Y')
ax.set_title('Data with Uncertainty \n')
ax.legend(loc='right', fontsize=12)

plt.show()
```

### Goals in uncertainty quantification?

There are four properties we should consider when building prediction intervals:
 
**Validity** - Our goal in using uncertainty quantification is to create valid prediction regions, such as intervals for regression or sets for classification, that guarantee our probabilistic statements are accurate. For example, if we have a prediction interval with 80 percent coverage, it means that there must be at least an 80 percent probability that the actual value falls within that interval. This is crucial for effectively quantifying uncertainty, as failing to meet this requirement undermines the fundamental purpose of the prediction.

**Verifying Coverage** - We need to consider robustness when creating prediction intervals from finite datasets. Due to the limited nature of our datasets, we often require prediction intervals that maintain coverage validity even with a small dataset; this property is known as finite-sample coverage validity.

**Efficiency** - Efficiency refers to how tight a prediction interval is. The tighter the 
interval, the more efficient it is. For example, a prediction of 100 units ± 5 is more 
efficient than 100 units ± 50, because a narrower interval gives us a tighter interval for decision making. When assessing intervals, it's important that we account for both coverage validity and efficiency.

**Model-agnostic and Distribution-Free** - An effective interval predictor should not rely on the particularities of the point predictor or data distribution. It should be able to work with any model, without requiring an understanding of the model's internal mechanisms.

With these properties in mind, we can now consider the general approaches to uncertainty quantification.

### Approaches to uncertainty

There are several approaches, with their own strengths and weaknesses. 

* Statistical - relies on probability distributions to model data and prediction uncertainties.
* Bayesian - relies on prior knowledge about data to update beliefs about prediction uncertainties. 
* Fuzzy logic - represents uncertainty using membership functions and fuzzy sets, allowing partial set membership. 

While all useful, they have their place and problems. Bayesian statistics, which is commonly discussed for UQ, is useful when one has thoroughly investigated the data/topic to be modelled, but its reliance on prior distributions is a problem for us when we are modelling hundreds, or more, items. It is simply not practical to properly investigate priors at scale, and not doing so often results in oversimplified or poorly selected priors, and therefore poor UQ. There is also the issue with Bayesian methods of being computationally expensive.

Fuzzy logic simply lacks the precision needed for many business tasks, such as forecasting. Generally, for business applications, statistical methods (i.e. regression analysis, confidence intervals, and Monte Carlo simulations) strike a better balance between efficiency and accurate uncertainty representation. Statistical approaches are more useful, but rely on parametric assumptions and computationally intensive methods like Monte Carlo bootstrapping.  

A newer non-parametric statistical approach to UQ is conformal prediction, which tends to perform well in business contexts due to its ability to provide precise probabilistic intervals which are distribution-free, model-agnostic, and generally computationally efficient [@manokhinPracticalGuideApplied2023]. Its key properties include:

* Non-parametric and distribution agnostic - saving us the need to investigate every model's data set fully 
* Probabilistic predictions with confidence metrics to support informed decisions
* Valid prediction regions - enabling rigorous uncertainty assessment [@angelopoulosConformalPredictionGentle2023]
* Maintains validity regardless of dataset size
* Robust to imperfect and noisy data
* Computationally efficient
* Applicable to most supervised learning

## Introduction to conformal prediction

Conformal prediction has a few requirements, the most important being that data is exchangeable. A sequence of random variables is exchangeable if their joint distribution is invariant to any permutation of the indices; the order does not matter for the joint distribution, but unlike IID the variables can be dependent. This is a slightly weaker assumption than the standard IID imposed in most machine learning.

A prediction region is said to be valid if a true value falls within the region at least $(1 - \alpha)$% of the time. The efficiency of a prediction region is its size: for classification, this is the number of classes in the set; for regression, it is the interval width. Under exchangeability, split conformal prediction gives us a finite-sample guarantee:

$$
P(Y_{n+1} \in C(X_{n+1})) \geq 1 - \alpha
$$

where $C(X_{n+1})$ is the prediction region for a new test point $X_{n+1}$, and $\alpha$ is the desired miscoverage rate. If we set $\alpha = 0.1$, our intervals will contain the true value at least 90% of the time.

In its purest form, conformal prediction is designed for an online (transductive) setting. This means that once a label is predicted in a supervised learning scenario, its true label is revealed before the next prediction is made. The underlying model can therefore be retrained with this newly acquired data point, and the next prediction is made using a calibration set containing $n + 1$ data points, where the previous model had $n$.

## Intuition behind conformal prediction

Consider the concept of point prediction we discussed earlier. Instead of providing a single value, we can use historical data to predict a range or interval of values. We then assign a probability to this interval, indicating the likelihood of the actual value/s we are trying to predict falling within it. For example, in our earlier regression problem, we used historical pairs of X and Y values to create a model, which allowed us to predict values of Y given a chosen value of X. Under uncertainty quantification with conformal intervals, we simply create a range of plausible values by using *actual data* to gauge the fit of our predictions. Any newly predicted points may align, or not, with our plausible range of previously observed values. Put simply, conformal intervals (or sets when talking about classification) help us find values that sufficiently conform with expectations based on our historical data, while values that do not conform are flagged as outliers.

To build a set of plausible values, we need a function that can measure how well a single value fits within a group of values. This is relatively trivial, as we can simply use distance metrics like absolute error (AE) to measure the non-conformity of a value. Of course, we could use many other measures (i.e. squared error, standardised residuals, Huber loss etc). The point is to apply a function that takes a single point and a group of points as input and calculates the distance/non-conformity between them.

To make this salient, we’ll apply a simple regression (below), with most points falling onto a linear path; then use the resulting model to predict a couple of points. 

```{python}
#| label: fig-regression-nonconformity
#| fig-cap: "Distance (absolute error) from a linear model. Two predicted points are highlighted: one close to the regression line with low absolute error (green), and one far from it with high absolute error (red)."
#| fig-alt: "Scatter plot with regression line showing two predicted points and their absolute errors as dashed vertical lines"
#| message: false
#| echo: false
#| eval: true

# linear regression
plt.style.use('seaborn-v0_8-ticks')
np.random.seed(235987)
x = np.linspace(0, 10, 30)
y = 2 * x + 1 + np.random.normal(scale=2, size=30)

# Fit regression 
p, residuals, _, _, _ = np.polyfit(x, y, 1, full=True)
fitted_y = np.poly1d(p)(x)

# calculate Abs error
absolute_errors = np.abs(y - fitted_y)

# Find indices and select based on rank
sorted_indices = np.argsort(absolute_errors)
point_near_index = sorted_indices[3]  # 4th lowest AE
point_far_index = sorted_indices[-2]  # 2nd highest AE

# Get idx values for highlighting
point_near = (x[point_near_index], y[point_near_index])
point_far = (x[point_far_index], y[point_far_index])
fitted_near = fitted_y[point_near_index]
fitted_far = fitted_y[point_far_index]

# calc AE for highlighted points
ae_near = absolute_errors[point_near_index]
ae_far = absolute_errors[point_far_index]

# Plotting
fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.plot(x, y, 'o', label='Data points', color=custom_palette[6])
ax.plot(x, fitted_y, '--', label='Regression Line', color=custom_palette[1])
ax.scatter(*point_near, color=custom_palette[3], label='Predicted point (low AE)', zorder=5)
ax.scatter(*point_far, color=custom_palette[2], label='Predicted point (high AE)', zorder=5)

# Draw AE lines
ax.plot([point_near[0], point_near[0]], [point_near[1], fitted_near], 'g--')
ax.plot([point_far[0], point_far[0]], [point_far[1], fitted_far], 'r--')

# Annotation etc
ax.text(point_near[0] - 0.1, (point_near[1] + fitted_near) / 1.7, f'AE: {ae_near:.2f}', ha='right')
ax.text(point_far[0] -0.1, (point_far[1] + fitted_far) / 1.85, f'AE: {ae_far:.2f}', ha='right')
ax.set_xlabel('X values (i.e. chocolate sales)')
ax.set_ylabel('Y values (i.e. Field medals)')
ax.set_title('Distance (absolute error) from linear model')
ax.legend()
plt.grid(True)
plt.show()
```

You can see the conformity of our predicted points to the interval/set (all points here) varies; the predicted point closest to the line appears to conform better to the entire data set. While our other prediction stands out in distance from most of the other points in the data set. Of course, distance doesn’t just apply to the predictions, but all the points within the interval, which all exhibit differing values of non-conformity, any function we select should measure these differences. A simple approach is to fit a linear OLS model and use it to measure absolute error ($AE = y-\hat{y}$) as our non-conformity metric (you can use other metrics). As you can see in our example, the predicted point closest to the line has the lowest absolute error of the two predictions. 

Simply establishing a difference between values and modelled expectations is not sufficient for determining conformity. Being a relative concept, conformity depends on the degree data in a set *conforms*. We resolve this by ranking values within a set by their respective distance (non-conformity); in essence, we create a *ranking/conformity ladder*, ordering conformity values from higher (low AE) to lower (high AE). Finally, we scale these ranking values to a standard 0-1 range.

To demonstrate this, we take a simple regression dataset, highlight one data point (shown in violet), and observe where it sits on the conformity ladder. We then shift that same point to an extreme position and see how its ranking changes, and how rankings of all other points shift as well. 

```{python}
#| label: fig-conformity-ladder-data
#| message: false
#| echo: false
#| eval: true

# Data
np.random.seed(42)
x = np.random.normal(50, 15, 20)
y_noisy = x * 3 + np.random.normal(0, 20, 20) 

# OLS regression 
model_noisy = LinearRegression().fit(x.reshape(-1,1), y_noisy)
y_pred_noisy = model_noisy.predict(x.reshape(-1, 1))
abs_errors_noisy = np.abs(y_pred_noisy - y_noisy)

# Increase sales value of one point 
x_modified = x.copy()
y_modified = y_noisy.copy()
y_modified[10] += 50  # 11th data has 50 added to it

# We recalculate preds and errors 
y_pred_modified = model_noisy.predict(x_modified.reshape(-1, 1))
abs_errors_modified = np.abs(y_pred_modified - y_modified)

# Calc conformity scores for both scenarios


conformity_scores_noisy = 1 - np.argsort(np.argsort(abs_errors_noisy)) / len(abs_errors_noisy)
conformity_scores_modified = 1 - np.argsort(np.argsort(abs_errors_modified)) / len(abs_errors_modified)

# Adj colours for ladder ranking (green down to red) based on conformity scores
sorted_conformity_scores_noisy = np.sort(conformity_scores_noisy)
sorted_conformity_scores_modified = np.sort(conformity_scores_modified)

sorted_indices_noisy = np.argsort(-conformity_scores_noisy)
sorted_indices_modified = np.argsort(-conformity_scores_modified)

colors_noisy = [sns.color_palette("RdYlGn_r", n_colors=len(abs_errors_noisy))[i] for i in range(len(abs_errors_noisy))]
colors_modified = [sns.color_palette("RdYlGn_r", n_colors=len(abs_errors_modified))[i] for i in range(len(abs_errors_modified))]

# Highlighting the modified point in the ladder
highlight_index = 10
highlight_color = "violet"
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-conformity-ladder
#| fig-cap: "Conformity ranking ladders for original and modified data. Top row: a data point (violet) sits near the regression line and ranks highly. Bottom row: the same point is shifted to an extreme position, dropping its ranking and shifting all others."
#| fig-alt: "Four-panel figure comparing scatter plots with regression lines alongside horizontal bar charts of conformity rankings"
#| message: false
#| echo: false
#| eval: true

# Plotting the corrected visualizations with highlights
fig, ax = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(18, 16))

# Original noisy data with regression line
sns.scatterplot(x=x, y=y_noisy, ax=ax[0,0], color="black", label="Data Points")
sns.scatterplot(x=[x[10]], y=[y_noisy[10]], ax=ax[0,0], color="violet", s=100, label="Predicted value")
sns.lineplot(x=x, y=y_pred_noisy, ax=ax[0,0], color="blue", label="Regression line average predicted value")
ax[0,0].set_title("Original Noisy Data with Regression Line")
ax[0,0].set_xlabel("Price discount", fontsize=16)
ax[0,0].set_ylabel("Sales", fontsize=16)
ax[0,0].legend(fontsize=16)

# Conformity ranking ladder for original noisy data
bar_colors_noisy = colors_noisy.copy()
bar_colors_noisy[sorted_indices_noisy.tolist().index(highlight_index)] = highlight_color
ax[0,1].barh(np.arange(len(conformity_scores_noisy)), abs_errors_noisy[sorted_indices_noisy], color=bar_colors_noisy)
ax[0,1].set_yticks(np.arange(len(conformity_scores_noisy)))
ax[0,1].set_yticklabels(np.round(conformity_scores_noisy[sorted_indices_noisy], 2))
ax[0,1].set_title("Conformity Ranking Ladder for Original Data")
ax[0,1].set_xlabel("Non-Conformity (Absolute Error)")
ax[0,1].set_ylabel("Conformity Ranking")
ax[0,1].invert_yaxis()

# Modified data with regression line
sns.scatterplot(x=x_modified, y=y_modified, ax=ax[1,0], color="black", label="Data Points")
sns.scatterplot(x=[x_modified[10]], y=[y_modified[10]], ax=ax[1,0], color="violet", s=100, label="Predicted Point")
sns.lineplot(x=x_modified, y=y_pred_modified, ax=ax[1,0], color="blue", label="Regression line extreme predicted value")
ax[1,0].set_title("Modified Data with Regression Line")
ax[1,0].set_xlabel("Price discount", fontsize=16)
ax[1,0].set_ylabel("Sales", fontsize=16)
ax[1,0].legend(fontsize=16)

# Conformity ranking ladder for modified data
bar_colors_modified = colors_modified.copy()
bar_colors_modified[sorted_indices_modified.tolist().index(highlight_index)] = highlight_color
ax[1,1].barh(np.arange(len(conformity_scores_modified)), abs_errors_modified[sorted_indices_modified], color=bar_colors_modified)
ax[1,1].set_yticks(np.arange(len(conformity_scores_modified)))
ax[1,1].set_yticklabels(np.round(conformity_scores_modified[sorted_indices_modified], 2))
ax[1,1].set_title("Conformity Ranking Ladder for Modified Data")
ax[1,1].set_xlabel("Non-Conformity (Absolute Error)")
ax[1,1].set_ylabel("Conformity Ranking")
ax[1,1].invert_yaxis()

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

Our ladder graphs above show how distance (non-conformity metric) from the simple regression (blue line) affects the position (conformity value) of our predicted values within the set, highlighting relative conformity. By scaling the values we have created a conformity metric from the rankings, based on non-conformity, between 0 and 1. This allows us to assign a confidence level ($\delta$; delta) for what we consider to be a sufficient threshold of conformity, defining which values from our data are accepted, or not, into a conformal interval based on a probability range with coverage. Specifically, when we set a $\delta$ threshold to 0.8, it will include values within the top 80% of our ranking ladder, with values outside (higher) than this considered non-conforming.

Now that we have the intuition, we can express it formally. The notation for this may looks dense, but each piece maps directly to what we've already discussed: $B$ is our set of data points, $F$ is our non-conformity function (absolute error in our examples), and $\Gamma$ is a prediction set, a collection of values that conform sufficiently.

Formally, when given a confidence threshold ($\delta$) and a set of paired training values $(x_0, y_0), \ldots, (x_{n-1}, y_{n-1})$, with a non-conformity function ($F$), a conformal prediction set consists of all plausible values for which the fraction of points conforming better than the test point is less than our confidence delta [adapted from @radUncertaintyQuantificationUsing2023].

Set of examples - 
$$ 
B \triangleq \left\{ (x_0, y_0), \ldots, (x_{n-1}, y_{n-1}), (x_n, y) \right\}
$$

Non-conformity scores for training values and test point – 
$$ 
f_i \triangleq F(B, (x_i, y_i)), \quad \forall i \in \{0, \ldots, n-1\}
$$

$$
f_n \triangleq F(B, (x_n, y)) 
$$

Prediction set – 
$$ \Gamma(x_0, y_0, \ldots, x_{n-1}, y_{n-1}, x_n) = \{y \in Y : |\{n \mid i = 1, \ldots, n : \hat{f}_i < \hat{f}_n\}| / n < \delta\}
 $$

Probability condition - 
$$ 
P(y_n \in \Gamma) \geq \delta 
$$


Under the assumption of exchangeability, this ensures that the probability of an actual value falling within our constructed interval is at least 80%. We can, therefore, state with 80% confidence that the actual value lies within our predicted interval. This is referred to as an 80% prediction interval. We are acknowledging the possibility of error in our predictions and quantifying that by stating there is less than a 20% chance of being incorrect, considering all plausible values that have the possibility of being assigned.

A prediction set consists of all plausible values that, when assigned to new data points, will make them conform sufficiently with the previously established points within the set. We define a sufficient conformity threshold $\delta$ on the standardised rankings of our conformity ladder, i.e. $\delta = 0.8$ means 80%, which will include values in the set below this threshold [adapted from @radUncertaintyQuantificationUsing2023].

::: {.callout-note}
## A note on notation
Throughout the intuition sections above we have used $\delta$ for our confidence level, following @radUncertaintyQuantificationUsing2023 — so $\delta = 0.8$ means we want 80% coverage. In conformal prediction literature and in libraries like MAPIE, you will commonly see $\alpha$ for *miscoverage* rate, where $\alpha = 1 - \delta$. A $\delta = 0.8$ confidence level is the same as $\alpha = 0.2$. We switch to $\alpha$ notation from the applied sections onward, as this is what the code expects.
:::

## Improving conformal intervals 
Up to here, we have learnt about full conformal prediction, which is of little benefit to us for two reasons... 
   
1. It is computationally expensive! Because whenever a new data point is tested we have to refit the underlying model for all points in the set used to build our non-conformity values. 
   
2. When we move a validation point with an unknown Y from a conforming position to an extreme non-conforming position, it distorts the fit of the underlying model. This impacts the non-conformity values, which consequently changes the values in our conformity ladder, impacting our measurements of plausible values.

```{python}
#| label: fig-full-conformal-refit
#| fig-cap: "The refitting problem in full conformal prediction. Top row: a data point (violet) sits at a reasonable position with a stable polynomial fit. Bottom row: the same point is shifted to an extreme position, distorting the fit and reshuffling the conformity ladder."
#| fig-alt: "Four-panel figure showing how moving a validation point changes the polynomial regression fit and conformity rankings"
#| message: false
#| echo: false
#| eval: true

np.random.seed(39285)
plt.rcParams['font.size'] = 20  
plt.rcParams['axes.labelsize'] = 20  
plt.rcParams['axes.titlesize'] = 20  
plt.rcParams['xtick.labelsize'] = 20 
plt.rcParams['ytick.labelsize'] = 20 

# synthetic data
x = np.random.normal(50, 15, 20)
y_noisy = x * 3 + np.random.normal(0, 20, 20)

# Polynomial transformation
poly = PolynomialFeatures(degree=3)
x_poly = poly.fit_transform(x.reshape(-1,1))

# Polynomial regression model
model_noisy = LinearRegression().fit(x_poly, y_noisy)
y_pred_noisy = model_noisy.predict(x_poly)
abs_errors_noisy = np.abs(y_pred_noisy - y_noisy)

# Identify highest x value index
highest_x_index = np.argmax(x)

# Modify y-value associated with highest x value
x_modified = x.copy()
y_modified = y_noisy.copy()
adjustment_value = 120  
y_modified[highest_x_index] += adjustment_value  # Add to make it higher

# Fit polynomial regression model on modified data
x_poly_modified = poly.transform(x_modified.reshape(-1, 1))
model_modified = LinearRegression().fit(x_poly_modified, y_modified)
y_pred_modified = model_modified.predict(x_poly_modified)
abs_errors_modified = np.abs(y_pred_modified - y_modified)

# Calculate conformity values
conformity_scores_noisy = np.argsort(np.argsort(abs_errors_noisy)) / (len(abs_errors_noisy) - 1)
conformity_scores_modified = np.argsort(np.argsort(abs_errors_modified)) / (len(abs_errors_modified) - 1)

# Colors for conformity ranking
norm_noisy = plt.Normalize(conformity_scores_noisy.min(), conformity_scores_noisy.max())
sorted_indices_noisy = np.argsort(conformity_scores_noisy)
colors_noisy = plt.cm.RdYlGn_r(norm_noisy(conformity_scores_noisy[sorted_indices_noisy]))

norm_modified = plt.Normalize(conformity_scores_modified.min(), conformity_scores_modified.max())
sorted_indices_modified = np.argsort(conformity_scores_modified)
colors_modified = plt.cm.RdYlGn_r(norm_modified(conformity_scores_modified[sorted_indices_modified]))

highlight_index = highest_x_index  
highlight_color = "violet"  

# Plotting
fig, ax = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(18, 16))

# Regression line with reasonable prediction
sns.scatterplot(x=x, y=y_noisy, ax=ax[0,0], color="black", label="Data Points")
sns.scatterplot(x=[x[highest_x_index]], y=[y_noisy[highest_x_index]], ax=ax[0,0], color="violet", s=100, label="High Price Data Point")
sns.lineplot(x=np.sort(x), y=model_noisy.predict(poly.transform(np.sort(x).reshape(-1, 1))), ax=ax[0,0], color="blue", label="Polynomial Regression Line")
ax[0,0].set_title("Regression line with reasonable prediction")
ax[0,0].set_xlabel("Price discount")
ax[0,0].set_ylabel("Sales")
ax[0,0].legend(fontsize=16)

# Conformity ladder for reasonable prediction
bar_colors_noisy = [highlight_color if i == highlight_index else colors_noisy[j] for j, i in enumerate(sorted_indices_noisy)]
ax[0,1].barh(np.arange(len(conformity_scores_noisy)), abs_errors_noisy[sorted_indices_noisy], color=bar_colors_noisy)
ax[0,1].set_yticks(np.arange(len(conformity_scores_noisy)))
ax[0,1].set_yticklabels(np.round(conformity_scores_noisy[sorted_indices_noisy], 2))
ax[0,1].set_title("Conformity ladder with reasonable prediction")
ax[0,1].set_xlabel("Non-Conformity (Absolute Error)")
ax[0,1].set_ylabel("Conformity Ranking")
ax[0,1].invert_yaxis()

# Regression line with extreme prediction
sns.scatterplot(x=x_modified, y=y_modified, ax=ax[1,0], color="black", label="Data Points")
sns.scatterplot(x=[x_modified[highest_x_index]], y=[y_modified[highest_x_index]], ax=ax[1,0], color="violet", s=100, label="Modified High Price Data Point")
sns.lineplot(x=np.sort(x_modified), y=model_modified.predict(poly.transform(np.sort(x_modified).reshape(-1, 1))), ax=ax[1,0], color="blue", label="New Polynomial Regression Line")
ax[1,0].set_title("Regression line with extreme prediction")
ax[1,0].set_xlabel("Price discount")
ax[1,0].set_ylabel("Sales")
ax[1,0].legend(fontsize=16)

# Conformity ladder for extreme prediction
bar_colors_modified = [highlight_color if i == highlight_index else colors_modified[j] for j, i in enumerate(sorted_indices_modified)]
ax[1,1].barh(np.arange(len(conformity_scores_modified)), abs_errors_modified[sorted_indices_modified], color=bar_colors_modified)
ax[1,1].set_yticks(np.arange(len(conformity_scores_modified)))
ax[1,1].set_yticklabels(np.round(conformity_scores_modified[sorted_indices_modified], 2))
ax[1,1].set_title("Conformity Ladder with extreme prediction")
ax[1,1].set_xlabel("Non-Conformity (Absolute Error)")
ax[1,1].set_ylabel("Conformity Ranking")
ax[1,1].invert_yaxis()

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

::: {.callout-note}
In our code, we use a raw rank convention where higher scores mean *less* conforming (higher error), which is the inverse of our earlier ladder plots. Both conventions are common; what matters is consistency within each calculation.
:::

While the computational overhead in this example is small, refitting the model underlying conformity metrics makes the full approach scale poorly with larger datasets and more complex models. Of course, we only need to refit because we are using the whole set for building the model, including the validation point/s. Being data scientists we know a few ways around this... 

* Exclude the validation/test point when building the model –
  * Excluding the validation point from the build is a straightforward no-go, as it often leads to values in our set having lower non-conformity values (AE here) than validation points, increasing a validation point’s non-conformity. Makes the prediction set too narrow. 

* Split conformal methods
  * Machine learning practice for building models is to do a training-test split (don’t fix what isn’t broken!). All we do here is hold more points from the set we use for model fitting, also we call the splits training and calibration sets. 
  * We use a sample of values from our set to fit the underlying model; once this is built, we use the remaining values to construct a conformity ladder which we apply our confidence threshold to.

The split method, as we now know, measures conformity based on one fit of the underlying model, which means we can move validation points wherever we choose without impacting the underlying model and non-/conformity measures, which as a bonus reduces computational cost.

```{python}
#| label: fig-split-conformal
#| fig-cap: "Split conformal method. Training data (red crosses) is used to fit the polynomial regression, while calibration data (blue circles) is held out to compute non-conformity scores and build the conformity ladder."
#| fig-alt: "Two-panel figure showing training and calibration data with polynomial fit alongside the calibration conformity ladder"
#| message: false
#| echo: false
#| eval: true
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split

np.random.seed(39285)

plt.rcParams['font.size'] = 20  
plt.rcParams['axes.labelsize'] = 20  
plt.rcParams['axes.titlesize'] = 20  
plt.rcParams['xtick.labelsize'] = 20 
plt.rcParams['ytick.labelsize'] = 20 

# synthetic data
x = np.random.normal(50, 15, 20)  
y = x * 3 + np.random.normal(0, 20, 20)

# Split data into training and calibration sets
x_train, x_calib, y_train, y_calib = train_test_split(x, y, test_size=0.4, random_state=42)

# Polynomial transformation for both sets
poly = PolynomialFeatures(degree=3)
x_train_poly = poly.fit_transform(x_train.reshape(-1,1))
x_calib_poly = poly.transform(x_calib.reshape(-1, 1))

# Fit polynomial regression model using training data
model = LinearRegression().fit(x_train_poly, y_train)

# Predict on both training and calibration sets
y_train_pred = model.predict(x_train_poly)
y_calib_pred = model.predict(x_calib_poly)

# Calculate absolute errors on calibration data to form conformity scores
abs_errors_calib = np.abs(y_calib_pred - y_calib)

# Sort ranking for conformity scores: 0 for lowest AE and 1 for highest AE
ranks = np.argsort(np.argsort(abs_errors_calib))
conformity_scores = ranks / (len(abs_errors_calib) - 1)

# Sorting indices for calibration data based on conformity scores
sorted_indices_calib = np.argsort(conformity_scores)  

# Plotting
fig, axs = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(16, 8))

# Training data with polynomial regression line
sns.scatterplot(x=x_train, y=y_train, ax=axs[0], marker='x', color=custom_palette[2], s=100, label="Training Data")
sns.scatterplot(x=x_calib, y=y_calib, ax=axs[0], marker='o', color=custom_palette[1], s=100, label="Calibration Data")
sns.lineplot(x=np.linspace(min(x), max(x), 100), y=model.predict(poly.transform(np.linspace(min(x), max(x), 100).reshape(-1, 1))), ax=axs[0], color="green", label="Polynomial Regression Line")
axs[0].set_title("Training and Calibration Data with Polynomial Fit")
axs[0].set_xlabel("Price discount")
axs[0].set_ylabel("Sales")
axs[0].legend(fontsize = 16)

# Conformity ladder for calibration data
norm = plt.Normalize(conformity_scores.min(), conformity_scores.max())
colors = plt.cm.RdYlGn_r(norm(conformity_scores[sorted_indices_calib]))  # Use reversed colormap
axs[1].barh(np.arange(len(conformity_scores)), abs_errors_calib[sorted_indices_calib], color=colors)
axs[1].set_yticks(np.arange(len(conformity_scores)))
axs[1].set_yticklabels(np.round(conformity_scores[sorted_indices_calib], 2))
axs[1].set_title("Conformity Ladder for Calibration Data")
axs[1].set_xlabel("Non-Conformity (Absolute Error)")
axs[1].set_ylabel("Conformity Ranking")
axs[1].invert_yaxis() 

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

### Conformal intervals with quartiles

Now let's say we have a validation point with an unknown value. If we use an 80% prediction interval, this means there is an 80% probability that an interval will contain the true value around that point; specifically the interval consists of all plausible values that, if assigned to the test point, will put it within top 80% of the conformity ladder.

How can we construct this interval? Rather than attempting a brute force approach and trying to generate all plausible values, we can take an intelligent approach, which is allowed for two reasons…
* We only need to fit our model once with a split approach
* There is a monotonic relationship between the distance of the prediction point and the model; meaning that as a point moves away from a model, its non-conformity increases, dropping its conformity ranking.

Using these properties we can find the upper and lower bounds of an interval, that deem acceptable. We only need to create subintervals of the rankings from our calibration set, which we use for the values in the conformity ladder. 


```{python}
#| label: fig-quartile-intervals
#| fig-cap: "Conformity ladder divided into quartile sub-intervals. Red dashed lines mark the boundaries at confidence levels 0.2, 0.4, 0.6, and 0.8."
#| fig-alt: "Horizontal bar chart of conformity scores with red dashed lines marking quartile boundaries"
#| message: false
#| echo: false
#| eval: true

# synthetic data
x = np.random.normal(50, 15, 30)  
y = x * 3 + np.random.normal(0, 20, 30)

# Split data into training and calibration sets
x_train, x_calib, y_train, y_calib = train_test_split(x, y, test_size=0.4, random_state=42)

# Polynomial transformation for both sets
poly = PolynomialFeatures(degree=3)
x_train_poly = poly.fit_transform(x_train.reshape(-1, 1))
x_calib_poly = poly.transform(x_calib.reshape(-1, 1))

# Fit polynomial regression model using only training data
model = LinearRegression().fit(x_train_poly, y_train)

# Predict on both training and calibration sets
y_train_pred = model.predict(x_train_poly)
y_calib_pred = model.predict(x_calib_poly)

# Calculate absolute errors on calibration data to form conformity scores
abs_errors_calib = np.abs(y_calib_pred - y_calib)

# Conformity scores: higher score = higher error = less conforming
# Note: this convention is inverted from earlier ladder plots,
# where we used (1 - rank/n) so that higher = more conforming.
# Both are valid; here we use the raw rank for the quartile construction.
ranks = np.argsort(np.argsort(abs_errors_calib))
conformity_scores = ranks / (len(abs_errors_calib) - 1)

# Sorting indices for calibration data based on conformity scores
sorted_indices_calib = np.argsort(conformity_scores) 

# Calculate quartiles - set quartiles here
quartile_bounds = np.percentile(conformity_scores, [20, 40, 60, 80]) 

# Conformity ladder for calibration data
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 8))
norm = plt.Normalize(conformity_scores.min(), conformity_scores.max())
colors = plt.cm.RdYlGn_r(norm(conformity_scores[sorted_indices_calib])) 
bars = ax.barh(np.arange(len(conformity_scores)), abs_errors_calib[sorted_indices_calib], color=colors)
ax.set_yticks(np.arange(len(conformity_scores)))
ax.set_yticklabels(np.round(conformity_scores[sorted_indices_calib], 2))
ax.set_title("Conformity Ladder for Calibration Data")
ax.set_xlabel("Non-Conformity (Absolute Error)")
ax.set_ylabel("Conformity Ranking")
ax.invert_yaxis()  

# Draw lines for quartile boundaries
for quartile in quartile_bounds:
    quartile_index = np.where(np.sort(conformity_scores) >= quartile)[0][0]
    quartile_position = len(conformity_scores) - quartile_index
    ax.axhline(y=quartile_position, color='r', linestyle='--')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

We built quartiles by simply selecting four points from our ladder to create five sub-intervals of the non-conformity score. These values correspond with confidence levels: 0.2, 0.4, 0.6, and 0.8. You can see that each sub-interval of conformal ranking ($Y$) corresponds to a range of non-conformities on the $X$. You can see that the highest probability corresponds to non-conformity scores between 0 and 6.39, the next interval scores are 6.4 and 8.62, etc. As the sub-intervals correspond to XY pairs, we can simply build a validation point with our exogenous (X) variable, get our prediction ($\hat{Y}$). Using the non-conformity sub-intervals, we can see where the prediction falls.

According to exchangeability, we should have equally probable sub-intervals, with the true unknown value for any validation point equally likely to fall within any of sub-intervals. As the example we used has five intervals, there is a 0.2 probability of a true value falling within any one of these intervals; in referring to 0.8 probability we are saying we will accept a value falling between 0 and 23 AE, which corresponds to 80% of our conformity ladder.

Our prediction intervals' width relative to confidence levels are discrete values determined by non-conformity scores and the size of a calibration dataset. Take our four calibration points, which restrict us to intervals of 20%, 40%, 60%, and 80%, and nothing else. As our confidence levels are not arbitrary, they are intrinsic and we denote them as $\delta_i$.

$$
\delta_j = \frac{j}{n+1} \quad \text{for } j = 1, \ldots, n
$$

Calibration set size: $n$

As non-conformity sub-intervals are equally probable, we can use the non-conformity scores as quantiles, which divide a probability distribution into equal probability intervals. 

$q^{\text{th}} \text{ Quantile} > q \times 100\% \text{ of All Possible Values}$

### Mathematical notation 

We now switch to the standard $\alpha$-based notation used in the literature and in libraries like MAPIE (recall from our earlier note that $\alpha = 1 - \delta$).
   
Prediction intervals for a new test point with prediction $\hat{y}$ are constructed by finding a non-conformity quantile $q$ at a desired confidence level and applying it symmetrically:

$$
C(\hat{y}) = \left[\hat{y} - q_{\lceil(n+1)(1-\alpha)\rceil / n}, \quad \hat{y} + q_{\lceil(n+1)(1-\alpha)\rceil / n}\right]
$$

where $q_k$ is the $k$-th smallest non-conformity score from the $n$ calibration points, with $k = \lceil(1-\alpha)(n+1)\rceil$.

Given the following quantiles
1.	q1/4 = 6.39
2.	q2/4 = 8.62
3.	q3/4 = 13.99
4.	q4/4 = 22.40

These values represent the quantiles of a distribution, specifically, the values below which a certain fraction of the data falls. For example, $q_{1/4}$ = 6.39 means that 25% of the data points are below this.

$$ 
q_{j/n} \quad \text{for } j = 1, 2, \ldots, n 
$$

Here we index quantiles by their position. With $j$ running over calibration points, and $n$ is the total number of calibration points. In our example $n$ = 4.

We connect these quartiles to the intrinsic confidence levels ($\delta_i$), where $\delta_i$ = 0.4 corresponds to 40% probability threshold. 

$$ 
\frac{q_{(j/n)}}{q_{((n+1)\delta'/n)}} 
$$

#### Step-by-step 

Below we walk through the calculation using the $\delta$-based confidence levels from our intuition section. Here $S$ is our non-conformity score function (absolute error, in our case), and $S^{-1}$ maps a non-conformity score back to a distance in the original Y scale. So if $S^{-1}(0.31) = 0.2$, it means a non-conformity score of 0.31 corresponds to a deviation of 0.2 units from the point prediction.

1. Take an intrinsic confidence level i.e. $𝛿𝑖$ = 0.4 (40%)
2. Number of calibration points i.e. $𝑛$ = 4
3. Apply the quartile formula, which measures non-conformity for the desired 𝛿𝑖:  
$$
q\left(\frac{(n+1)\delta'}{n}\right) : q\left(\frac{(4+1) \cdot 0.4}{4}\right) = q\left(\frac{2}{4}\right)
$$

1. This tells us that the confidence level of 0.4 corresponds to the specific quantile 2 (which we labelled B), which has a corresponding non-conformity quantile of $q\left(\frac{2}{4}\right) = 0.31$

2. The function $S^{-1}$  maps this non-conformity score back to the corresponding value in the original scale. Suppose $S^{-1}\left(0.31\right)$ which gives us a specific value, say ${\Delta y}$
3. The prediction interval is then constructed using this non-conformity value applied as the upper and lower of this value from the point prediction of the model. 
$$\{\hat{y} - \Delta y_{\max}, \hat{y} + \Delta y_{\min}\} = \{\hat{y} - S^{-1}(0.31), \hat{y} + S^{-1}(0.31)\}
$$

In our example 
$\{2.5-0.2,2.5+0.2\}=\{2.3,2.7\}$
7. The coverage probability for the specified confidence interval should be at least equal to the request $𝛿𝑖$

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## Arbitrary confidence levels
We can’t simply take two intervals and work out the value between our established intervals, as we cannot be confident that the upper and lower intervals have equal values in the non-conformity space. Instead, we select the highest sub-interval, so say we have intervals of 40 and 60%, but we want to use 𝛿 = 0.5 we select the subinterval for 60%. 

$$
\{\hat{y}-S^{-1}\left[q_{\left\lceil\left(n+1\right)\delta/n\right\rceil}\right],\hat{y}+S^{-1}\left[q_{\left\lceil\left(n+1\right)\delta/n\right\rceil}\right]\}
$$

This expands quartiles to a ceiling while removing the desired intrinsic value from our formula, creating a wider confidence interval than originally desired. This protects us from violating the coverage validity. But compared to the desired probability, how much higher is the actual probability?

In this upper bound, we can focus on sub-intervals $3/4$ and $4/4$ only. The worst-case scenario occurs when we want to use a confidence level ($\delta$) that is just above an intrinsic 
confidence level, for example 60.1%. This forces us to extend a prediction interval by almost an entire sub-interval to reach the next intrinsic confidence level.

The probability associated with one full sub-interval is $1/(n+1)$, as there are $n+1$ equiprobable sub-intervals. Including an entire sub-interval, which we need to do when the requested $\Delta$ is just above an intrinsic confidence level, would increase the coverage probability by $1/(n+1)$. Thus, the upper bound for the requested $\Delta$ is:

$$ 
\Delta + \frac{1}{n+1} 
$$ 

The upper probability bound found in the literature may not be the most precise. When adding $1/(n+1)$ to the requested $\Delta$, it assumes $\Delta$ is only slightly above one of the intrinsic confidence levels. 

$$
\delta \le P \left(y^{\mathrm{true}}\in\mathrm{Interval} \right)\le\delta+\frac{1}{n+1}
$$

However, consider another scenario where $\Delta$ is just below one of our intrinsic confidence levels, for example, 79.9%. Reaching the next intrinsic width (80%) does not require a complete sub-interval adjustment. Instead, we only need a small adjustment to reach the next intrinsic level. We can modify the right-hand side of the inequality to create a more precise new upper bound that is smaller or equal to the old one but never greater.

$$
\delta\ \le\ P\left(y^{\mathrm{true}}\ \in\ \mathrm{Interval} \right)\ \le\ \frac{\left\lceil\ \left(n\ +\ 1\right)\delta\ \right\rceil}{n\ +\ 1}
$$
:::

## Approaches to split

We have discussed using a single split to train the underlying model to calibrate conformal rankings. But as is true in most statistical learning procedures, there are approaches to splitting and training data, which can add robustness to our UQ.  

* Bootstrapping
* Cross-conformal – essentially K-fold splitting
* Jackknife 

Under cross-conformal, data is split into sections, i.e. $k = 4$. The first three pieces are combined to train an underlying model, and non-conformity scores are computed for the remaining section. We repeat this process for each fold, ensuring all data is used as a calibration set, which gives us non-conformity scores for the entire dataset and allows us to compute quantiles. 

We can take this idea further using a jackknife method, which is in essence leave-one-out validation, where 𝑛 models are trained, each with $𝑛 – 1$ data points. There are variations on the jackknife approach: jackknife+ and jackknife-minimax; with the former providing better coverage, while the latter is more conservative and quicker to run.  

* Jackknife+-after-Bootstrap combines the jackknife+ and bootstrap approaches - which involves resampling the training set with replacement, fitting regression functions on the bootstraps, and computing predictions on the complementary sets. This is an ensemble methodology that aggregates predictions to compute conformity scores, with reduced computational cost compared to jackknife+.

* Conformalized Quantile Regression (CQR) is particularly effective for heteroscedastic data. It uses quantile regressors to estimate conditional prediction bounds directly, rather than assuming a symmetric interval around the point prediction. Residuals from these regressors are then used to create guaranteed coverage intervals. CQR adapts to your data's variability, providing accurate interval widths where they matter.

See the [MAPIE](https://mapie.readthedocs.io/en/latest/theoretical_description_regression.html) - for more detail on these approaches.

### Which approach?
Single split is the least computationally intensive method. But it can result in higher variance in prediction sets, and suboptimal data use since it ignores variance from model refits. This works better with large data sets.

Of course, jackknife is computationally expensive. We would recommend using it when you need high precision, or model refitting is not expensive to you.

We typically find the cross-conformal approach (CV and other resampling methods) to strike a good balance between single split and jackknife methods.

## Conformalised regression 
Our primary goal in modelling uncertainty for regression tasks is to take our pre-trained models, e.g., RNN, GBDT, etc., and use them to forecast point predictions, then add uncertainty context to these. We could code this directly in Java, C, etc., or simply use a library like MAPIE or conformal-tights, which can both wrap around models whether they are PyTorch, TensorFlow, or scikit-learn.

The process for regression is straightforward — fit, predict, compute residuals, quantile,
interval. We'll apply this directly to time series in the next section.

1.	Fit model on training data.
2.	Make prediction on calibration data.
3.	Compute the absolute errors made by the model on calibration data.
4.	Get the ($1 - \alpha$) quantile from the distribution of absolute errors obtained at the previous point.
5.	Make prediction on test data.
6.	Compute left (right) end of the interval by subtracting (adding) the quantile obtained at point 4 to the predictions obtained at point 5.

## Conformalised forecasting 
So far, we have discussed regression modelling outside of time in order to build intuition and understanding. But to make conformal intervals applicable to time series data, we need to consider a critical issue: exchangeability. Time series data is not inherently exchangeable because the order of our points matters, observations at $t$ are typically dependent on observations at $t-1, t-2, \ldots$ in ways that permutation would destroy.

Several strategies exist to recover valid conformal intervals despite this. The common thread is constructing residuals that are approximately exchangeable, either by weighting recent observations more heavily, by using out-of-bag errors from bootstrap ensembles, or by aggregating across multiple models.
 
To get around this we can use weightings, relative to temporal relevance, error estimation from bootstrapped models, aggregation of predictions. 

In a simple weighting approach we calculate a prediction interval based on windows of observations, i.e. a fixed window giving temporal relevance to recent observations. Of course, while this ensures predictions resemble recent data, it might miss key factors like seasonality or exogenous variable influence like price. A better approach is to create an ensemble of multiple predictions, which can capture different aspects and trends within our data, and to use aggregations of the ensemble to reduce model-specific errors. 

* Simple weighted - follows a standard conformal prediction split and assigns weights to 
predictions based on temporal proximity, giving more influence to recent observations - [@angelopoulosConformalPredictionGentle2023]
* Ensemble Bootstrap Prediction Interval (EnbPI) – uses multiple bootstrap samples to train multiple versions of a model. It then combines predictions from these models and uses errors from a model's out-of-sample predictions to adjust prediction intervals - [@xuConformalPredictionTime2023]
  * There is an adaptive version (ACI) that updates the intervals online
* Sequential Conformal Prediction - [@xuSequentialPredictiveConformal2023]
* Hopfield Network (HopCT) – Applies a Hopfield network architecture to extract features from a time series and compute prediction errors. Errors are used to weight time-series values based on similarity to past errors. It is primarily used with multivariate data [@auerConformalPredictionTime2023]. 
* Bellman Conformal Inference (BCI) - formulates a stochastic control problem that minimises interval lengths while ensuring target coverage levels. Use dynamic programming to solve this problem, adjusting the trade-off between interval length and miscoverage. Mostly used for multistep forecasting, making it unsuitable for recursive modelling [@yangBellmanConformalInference2024].
 
EnbPI is a common approach at this time, and is what we will be using in this example, however we do recommend trying different approaches and libraries (i.e. PUNCC). EnbPI works with the following process:

-	Select a bootstrap ensemble estimator 
-	Fit $B$ ensemble estimators by bootstrapping from the training data $B$ times (generally 10-20). Each estimator is trained on a different bootstrap sample, resulting in $B$ unique models.
-	Compute residuals for each time point ($t = 1, \ldots, T$) using estimators that did not include time point $t$ in their training. These out-of-sample errors serve as a measure of prediction variance. 
-	Collect all errors into a single array.
-	Use an ensemble estimator to generate and aggregate (mean or median) predictions for test data.
-	Construct prediction intervals using aggregated predictions and a chosen confidence delta
-   Finally, select quantiles from the distribution of out-of-sample errors. 

![EnbPI process. Training data is bootstrapped B times to create B models. Each model produces out-of-bag predictions on time points it did not train on. Residuals from these predictions form the basis for conformal interval construction.](images/chap7_images/enbpi_pipeline.png){fig-align="centre" fig-alt="Figure 7.X: Flow diagram showing the EnbPI process split into training phase and prediction phase, converging at prediction interval construction"}

![Out-of-bag residual computation. For each training observation, only models that did not include it in their bootstrap sample contribute predictions. The aggregated prediction is compared to the true value to produce a residual.](images/chap7_images/enbpi_oob_residuals.png){fig-align="centre" fig-alt="Figure 7.X: Grid showing four models, four training observations, which models are excluded per observation, and the resulting OOB predictions and residuals"}

![From residuals to prediction intervals. The distribution of out-of-bag residuals provides the quantile that sets the interval half-width. Combined with aggregated point predictions from the full ensemble, this produces the final prediction intervals.](images/chap7_images/enbpi_residual_to_interval.png){fig-align="centre" fig-alt="Figure 7.X: Diagram showing residual distribution histogram with quantile marker converging with test predictions into the final interval formula"}

The key mechanism in EnbPI is how it computes out-of-bag residuals. For each training observation, only models that did *not* train on that observation produce predictions. These predictions are aggregated, and the absolute error against the true value becomes the residual for that time point. Because each residual comes from an out-of-sample prediction, these residuals are approximately exchangeable even though the underlying time series is not.

EnbPI achieves approximate exchangeability by computing residuals only from models that did not train on the corresponding time point. Because each residual comes from an out-of-sample prediction, these residuals are approximately exchangeable even though the underlying time series is not.

Under EnbPI, we need to be cautious when using larger training sets. While they can improve coverage guarantees, they can also slow down residual updates if the model isn't refitted. Balancing the number of training samples is crucial to fitting a model and updating prediction intervals effectively.

::: {.callout-tip}
## Forecasting Libraries
Libraries like Darts and NeuralForecast provide conformal prediction intervals directly in their forecasting pipelines, making the wrapping approach we show here unnecessary for those frameworks. We demonstrate the manual approach to build understanding of what these libraries do. Investigating the github code, and reconstructing it can also be worth while.
:::

### Applying EnbPI to our PyTorch model

We now apply EnbPI to a feedforward network trained on our pseudo sales data. The process involves several steps: loading and preparing the data, defining and training the PyTorch model, wrapping it for compatibility with MAPIE, and finally computing conformal prediction intervals.

We start by loading the data and creating windowed features, which we covered in earlier chapters.

```{python}
#| label: enbpi-data-loading
#| message: false
#| echo: true
#| eval: true

import os
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
from torch.utils.data import DataLoader, Dataset
import lightning as L
from lightning.pytorch.callbacks import EarlyStopping
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from sklearn.preprocessing import MinMaxScaler

device = torch.device("cuda" if torch.cuda.is_available() else "cpu")

data = pd.read_csv(CFG.data_folder / 'pseudo_sales.csv')
data['date'] = pd.to_datetime(data['date'], format='%d/%m/%Y')
data = data.sort_values('date').reset_index(drop=True)

def add_datetime_features(df, datetime_column):
    df = df.copy()
    df[datetime_column] = pd.to_datetime(df[datetime_column])
    df['year'] = df[datetime_column].dt.year
    df['month'] = df[datetime_column].dt.month
    df['week'] = df[datetime_column].dt.isocalendar().week.astype(int)
    df['day'] = df[datetime_column].dt.day
    return df

df = add_datetime_features(data, "date")

date_col = df['date'].copy()
target = df['sales'].copy()
features = df.drop(columns=['sales', 'date']).copy()

feature_scaler = MinMaxScaler()
target_scaler = MinMaxScaler()

features_scaled = feature_scaler.fit_transform(features)
target_scaled = target_scaler.fit_transform(target.values.reshape(-1, 1))

def create_time_windows(features, target, window_size):
    inputs, targets = [], []
    for i in range(len(features) - window_size):
        inputs.append(features[i:i + window_size])
        targets.append(target[i + window_size])
    return np.array(inputs), np.array(targets)

window_size = 32
inputs, targets = create_time_windows(features_scaled, target_scaled, window_size)
```

Next, we create the PyTorch dataset and split into training, validation, and test sets. Note that we preserve temporal order in the split, which is critical for time series.

```{python}
#| label: enbpi-dataset-splits
#| message: false
#| echo: true
#| eval: true

class TimeSeriesDataset(Dataset):
    def __init__(self, inputs, targets):
        self.inputs = torch.tensor(inputs, dtype=torch.float32)
        self.targets = torch.tensor(targets, dtype=torch.float32)

    def __len__(self):
        return len(self.inputs)

    def __getitem__(self, idx):
        return self.inputs[idx], self.targets[idx]

train_size = int(0.6 * len(inputs))
val_size = int(0.2 * len(inputs))

train_dataset = TimeSeriesDataset(inputs[:train_size], targets[:train_size])
val_dataset = TimeSeriesDataset(inputs[train_size:train_size + val_size], targets[train_size:train_size + val_size])
test_dataset = TimeSeriesDataset(inputs[train_size + val_size:], targets[train_size + val_size:])

train_loader = DataLoader(train_dataset, batch_size=32, shuffle=False)
val_loader = DataLoader(val_dataset, batch_size=32, shuffle=False)
test_loader = DataLoader(test_dataset, batch_size=32, shuffle=False)
```

We define the feedforward network using PyTorch Lightning. This is the same architecture used in Chapter 6, with hyperparameters from our Optuna tuning.

```{python}
#| label: enbpi-model-training
#| message: false
#| echo: true
#| eval: true

class FFNetwork(L.LightningModule):
    def __init__(self, input_dim, sequence_length, hidden_dim, 
                 num_layers=1, output_dim=1, learning_rate=0.0001, 
                 dropout_rate=0.5, activation_func=nn.ReLU()):
        super(FFNetwork, self).__init__()
        self.layers = nn.ModuleList(
            [nn.Linear(input_dim * sequence_length, hidden_dim)]
        )
        for _ in range(num_layers - 1):
            self.layers.append(nn.Linear(hidden_dim, hidden_dim))
        self.layers.append(nn.Linear(hidden_dim, output_dim))
        self.dropout = nn.Dropout(dropout_rate)
        self.activation = activation_func
        self.learning_rate = learning_rate

    def forward(self, x):
        batch_size, sequence_length, input_dim = x.size()
        x = x.view(batch_size, sequence_length * input_dim)
        for i in range(len(self.layers) - 1):
            x = self.activation(self.layers[i](x))
            x = self.dropout(x)
        return self.layers[-1](x)

    def training_step(self, batch, batch_idx):
        x, y = batch
        loss = nn.functional.mse_loss(self.forward(x), y)
        self.log('train_loss', loss)
        return loss

    def validation_step(self, batch, batch_idx):
        x, y = batch
        loss = nn.functional.mse_loss(self.forward(x), y)
        self.log('val_loss', loss)

    def configure_optimizers(self):
        return torch.optim.Adam(self.parameters(), lr=self.learning_rate)

input_dim = inputs.shape[2]
sequence_length = inputs.shape[1]

torch.manual_seed(755863)

model = FFNetwork(
    input_dim=input_dim, sequence_length=sequence_length,
    hidden_dim=63, num_layers=4, output_dim=1,
    learning_rate=0.000689, dropout_rate=0.170971
).to(device)

trainer = L.Trainer(
    accelerator="auto", devices=1, max_epochs=300,
    callbacks=[EarlyStopping(monitor='val_loss', patience=50, mode='min')],
    logger=True
)
trainer.fit(model, train_loader, val_loader)
```

::: {.callout-note}
Our hyperparameters above (`hidden_dim=63`, `learning_rate=0.000689`, `dropout_rate=0.170971`) were found using Optuna in a previous version of Chapter 6. See that chapter's notebook for tuning code.
:::

MAPIE expects a scikit-learn compatible estimator, so we need to wrap our PyTorch model. The wrapper exposes `fit` and `predict` methods while keeping the underlying model frozen. There is one subtlety here: MAPIE works with 2D arrays (samples, features), but our FFNetwork expects 3D input (samples, window_size, features). The wrapper handles this by reshaping the flattened input back to 3D before passing it to the model.

```{python}
#| label: enbpi-sklearn-wrapper
#| message: false
#| echo: true
#| eval: true
 
from sklearn.base import BaseEstimator, RegressorMixin
 
class SklearnWrapper(BaseEstimator, RegressorMixin):
    def __init__(self, model, window_size, n_features, device='cpu'):
        self.model = model
        self.window_size = window_size
        self.n_features = n_features
        self.device = device
        self.model.to(self.device)
 
    def fit(self, X, y):
        return self  # model is pre-trained
 
    def predict(self, X):
        self.model.eval()
        # MAPIE passes flattened 2D (samples, window*features)
        # FFNetwork expects 3D (samples, window, features)
        X_3d = X.reshape(-1, self.window_size, self.n_features)
        X_tensor = torch.tensor(X_3d, dtype=torch.float32).to(self.device)
        with torch.no_grad():
            predictions = self.model(X_tensor).cpu().numpy()
        return predictions.squeeze()
 
n_features = inputs.shape[2]
wrapped_model = SklearnWrapper(
    model, window_size=window_size, 
    n_features=n_features, device='cpu'
)
 
def extract_data_from_loader(loader):
    inputs_list, targets_list = [], []
    for batch in loader:
        inp, tgt = batch
        inputs_list.append(inp.cpu().numpy())
        targets_list.append(tgt.cpu().numpy())
    return np.vstack(inputs_list), np.concatenate(targets_list)
 
X_train, y_train = extract_data_from_loader(train_loader)
X_test, y_test = extract_data_from_loader(test_loader)
 
# Flatten windowed features for MAPIE's sklearn interface
# (samples, window_size, features) → (samples, window_size * features)
X_train = X_train.reshape(X_train.shape[0], -1)
X_test = X_test.reshape(X_test.shape[0], -1)
y_train = y_train.ravel()
y_test = y_test.ravel()
```

With our wrapper in place, we can apply MAPIE's `MapieTimeSeriesRegressor` with the EnbPI method. Block bootstrapping respects temporal structure by resampling contiguous blocks rather than individual observations.

```{python}
#| label: enbpi-conformal-intervals
#| message: false
#| echo: true
#| eval: true
 
from mapie.regression import TimeSeriesRegressor
from mapie.subsample import BlockBootstrap
 
confidence_level = 0.9  # 90% prediction intervals
 
cv_mapiets = BlockBootstrap(
    n_resamplings=30, n_blocks=30, overlapping=False, random_state=59
)
mapie_enbpi = TimeSeriesRegressor(
    wrapped_model, method="enbpi", cv=cv_mapiets, 
    agg_function="mean", n_jobs=1
)
mapie_enbpi.fit(X_train, y_train)
 
y_pred_enbpi, y_pis_enbpi = mapie_enbpi.predict(
    X_test, confidence_level=confidence_level, ensemble=True
)
```

### Evaluating conformal interval quality

Two metrics matter when evaluating conformal intervals: *coverage* (does the interval contain the true value at least $(1 - \alpha)$% of the time?) and *width* (how tight are the intervals?). A wide interval that always covers is useless; a tight interval that misses too often is dangerous.

```{python}
#| label: enbpi-evaluation
#| message: false
#| echo: true
#| eval: true
 
y_lower = y_pis_enbpi[:, 0, 0] 
y_upper = y_pis_enbpi[:, 1, 0]

in_interval = (y_test >= y_lower) & (y_test <= y_upper)
coverage = float(in_interval.mean())
width = float(np.mean(y_upper - y_lower))

print(f"Target coverage: {confidence_level:.0%}")
print(f"Achieved coverage: {coverage:.3f}")
print(f"Mean interval width: {width:.3f}")
```

Our output shows us the we have 0.915 coverage with an interval of 0.189, which is what we were looking for, lets see what that looks like:

```{python}
#| label: fig-enbpi-results
#| fig-cap: "EnbPI conformal intervals on the pseudo sales test set. The 90% prediction 
#|   interval is shaded, with points falling outside marked in orange. Misses cluster at 
#|   peaks where the model systematically underpredicts."
#| fig-alt: "Time series plot showing actual vs predicted sales with a shaded 90% 
#|   conformal prediction interval and orange markers for missed points"
#| message: false
#| echo: false
#| eval: true

y_test_orig = target_scaler.inverse_transform(y_test.reshape(-1, 1)).ravel()
y_pred_orig = target_scaler.inverse_transform(y_pred_enbpi.reshape(-1, 1)).ravel()
y_lower_orig = target_scaler.inverse_transform(y_pis_enbpi[:, 0, 0].reshape(-1, 1)).ravel()
y_upper_orig = target_scaler.inverse_transform(y_pis_enbpi[:, 1, 0].reshape(-1, 1)).ravel()

in_interval = (y_test_orig >= y_lower_orig) & (y_test_orig <= y_upper_orig)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(CFG.img_dim1, CFG.img_dim2))

ax.plot(y_test_orig, color=custom_palette[0], alpha=0.7, label="Actual")
ax.plot(y_pred_orig, color=custom_palette[1], label="Predicted")
ax.fill_between(
    range(len(y_test_orig)),
    y_lower_orig, y_upper_orig,
    alpha=0.2, color=custom_palette[5],
    label=f"{confidence_level:.0%} EnbPI interval"
)
ax.scatter(
    np.where(~in_interval)[0], y_test_orig[~in_interval],
    color=custom_palette[2], s=20, zorder=5, label="Missed"
)
ax.set_xlabel("Test time step")
ax.set_ylabel("Sales")
ax.set_title(f"EnbPI conformal intervals (coverage={coverage:.2f})")
ax.legend()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

The top panel shows our point predictions against actuals, with the 90% EnbPI interval shaded. Points falling outside our interval are marked in orange. Notice that we miss peak sales, sharp spikes where the model systematically underpredicts. This is a limitation of our underlying model rather than of conformal prediction itself. If our FFN doesn't capture peak behaviour well, the calibration residuals won't reflect those extremes adequately. The intervals are also symmetric ($\hat{y} \pm q$), so they can't adapt to directional bias. Conformalized Quantile Regression (CQR) addresses this by allowing asymmetric intervals, and is worth investigating when your model has systematic bias in one direction.

It is also worth noting that our intervals have constant width across all test points. This is because basic EnbPI uses a single quantile from the calibration residuals. Adaptive variants (AEnbPI) update the residual distribution as new observations arrive, producing intervals that widen or narrow in response to changing conditions, we leave this as an exercise for the companion notebook in the repository.


## Summary
In this chapter you have learnt that uncertainty quantification is critical for giving us confidence in our point predictions, and is a useful tool for tracking the quality of our forecasts. We introduced conformal intervals and built an intuitive understanding of how error metrics such as absolute error can be used to measure non-conformity to historical data relative to a regression model. You now understand that we rank the errors and standardise them to give us a probability measure that we can apply to our data, one that has guarantees about coverage and validity. Finally, you learnt how to apply this with a forecasting model by weighting ensembled predictions (EnbPI), which we applied to a PyTorch model. This is very much the start of the uncertainty quantification journey, but you now have the tools to break into this fast-moving and fascinating field of research.      


::: {.callout-tip}
## Reading list

* Angelopoulos, A.N. and Bates, S. — A Gentle Introduction to Conformal Prediction and 
  Distribution-Free Uncertainty Quantification 
  — <https://arxiv.org/abs/2107.07511>
* @manokhinPracticalGuideApplied2023 — Practical Guide to Applied Conformal Prediction 
  in Python
* @radUncertaintyQuantificationUsing2023 — MLBoost video lectures on conformal prediction 
  — <https://youtube.com/playlist?list=PLQkjeU22jN9tpbe7PpoA5nPG43SCgd7Sj>
* Taquet, V. et al. — MAPIE: an open-source library for distribution-free uncertainty 
  quantification — <https://towardsdatascience.com/with-mapie-uncertainties-are-back-in-machine-learning-882d5c17fdc3>
* MAPIE explained 
  — <https://towardsdatascience.com/mapie-explained-exactly-how-you-wished-someone-explained-to-you-78fb8ce81ff3>
* McBride Ellis, C. — Kaggle notebooks on conformal prediction with MAPIE 
  — [Prediction intervals](https://www.kaggle.com/code/carlmcbrideellis/regression-prediction-intervals-with-mapie) 
  | [Locally weighted](https://www.kaggle.com/code/carlmcbrideellis/locally-weighted-conformal-regression)
* Angelopoulos, A.N. — Conformal prediction notebooks 
  — <https://github.com/aangelopoulos/conformal-prediction/tree/main/notebooks>
* Comprehensive resource list 
  — <https://github.com/valeman/awesome-conformal-prediction>
:::
